mappings.rationaleAI: Oceania's geography including Australia, Melanesia, Micronesia, and Polynesia, Aboriginal and Polynesian settlement, and European exploration and colonization directly cover the character and historical development of the diverse peoples of Oceania (A), geography and ethnography (A.2), and European colonial settlement (A.3).

Australia's Aboriginal habitation, British colonization from 1788, Federation in 1901, participation in World Wars, and Indigenous land rights comprehensively cover early European exploration (B.1), British colonization and development (B.2), early commonwealth years (B.3), and relations with the Aboriginal population (B.4).

European maritime exploration connecting previously isolated world regions covers European exploration and colonial settlement in Oceania (A.3) and early European exploration of Australia by sea and land (B.1).
Polynesia's Austronesian migration, expert open-ocean navigation, and inter-island political histories directly cover the geography, ethnography, and prehistory of Polynesia (A.2).

The British Empire's Pacific exploration, dominion system, and colonial governance connect to European colonial settlement (A.3), British colonization of New South Wales (B.2), and British control over New Zealand (C.1).

European colonial domination through mercantilism and coercive institutions covers European exploration and colonial settlement including missionaries and trading societies (A.3) and British colonization of Australia (B.2).
The Pacific Ocean's island distribution, prehistoric Pacific migrations, and European exploration connect to the character of Oceania's peoples (A) and geography and ethnography of the region (A.2).
European extension of power over foreign territories covers the effects of colonization on Oceania's indigenous peoples through missionaries, trading societies, and colonial government (A.3).
